channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Strengthening Human-Centric Chain-of-Thought Reasoning Integrity in LLMs via a Structured Prompt Framework
item.description: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting enhances LLM reasoning but lacks verified integrity in security-critical tasks like threat detection. This paper introduces a lightweight, human-centric structured prompt framework—grounded in four dimensions: (1) Context & Scope Control, (2) Evidence Grounding & Traceability, (3) Reasoning Structure & Cognitive Control, and (4) Security-Specific Analytical Constraints—comprising 16 explicit, auditable reasoning controls to mitigate hallucination and drift. Evaluated on DDoS detection in SDN traffic across Llama-3, Qwen2, and Phi-3 models, the framework boosts accuracy by up to 40% in smaller models and delivers consistent gains across scales. Pareto analysis confirms superior trade-offs among accuracy, explainability, and latency; ablation studies highlight evidence grounding and security constraints as most critical for reducing hallucination (−57%) and reasoning drift (−63%). Human evaluation by cybersecurity experts (Cohen’s κ = 0.83) validates robust interpretability and auditability. The framework establishes structured prompting as a practical, transparent, and deployable foundation for trustworthy AI-driven cybersecurity analysis.

item.title: Forgetting to Witness: Efficient Federated Unlearning and Its Visible Evaluation
item.description: This paper introduces the first complete pipeline for *federated unlearning*, addressing the critical need for verifiable model forgetting under data deletion requests (e.g., GDPR’s “right to be forgotten”). We propose an efficient, history-free unlearning algorithm leveraging knowledge distillation and adaptive gradient masking—enabling accurate, single-round unlearning without storing raw client data or retraining from scratch. To objectively evaluate forgetting, we design **Skyeye**, a novel visualization framework that embeds the unlearned model as a classifier in a GAN; the generator, guided jointly by this classifier and a discriminator, synthesizes samples whose semantic relevance to the deleted data quantifies residual memorization. Experiments on CIFAR-10, FEMNIST, and CheXpert show our method achieves 98.2% forget accuracy with <3% extra communication overhead, while Skyeye reduces SSIM similarity to deleted classes by 74% versus baselines—providing the first interpretable, measurable, and deployable solution for trustworthy federated learning.

item.title: Your Agent, Their Asset: A Real-World Safety Analysis of OpenClaw
item.description: This paper presents the first real-world safety evaluation of OpenClaw—the most widely deployed personal AI agent in early 2026—operating with full local system access and integration into sensitive services (e.g., Gmail, Stripe, filesystem). We introduce the **CIK taxonomy**, unifying an agent’s persistent state into three orthogonal dimensions—**Capability**, **Identity**, and **Knowledge**—to enable systematic safety analysis beyond sandboxed benchmarks. Evaluating 12 attack scenarios across four backbone models (Claude Sonnet 4.5, Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4) on a live OpenClaw instance, we find that poisoning *any single CIK dimension* increases average attack success from 24.6% to 64–74%, with even the most robust model exhibiting >3× baseline vulnerability. Three CIK-aligned defenses and a file-protection mechanism were tested: the strongest defense still yields 63.8% success under Capability-targeted attacks, while file protection blocks 97% of malicious injections but also prevents legitimate updates. These results demonstrate that vulnerabilities are *architectural*, not incidental—necessitating foundational safeguards for personal AI agents. Code and benchmark: https://ucsc-vlaa.github.io/CIK-Bench.

item.title: Economic Security of VDF-Based Randomness Beacons: Models, Thresholds, and Design Guidelines
item.description: Verifiable Delay Function (VDF)-based randomness beacons promise bias-resistant, publicly verifiable randomness for blockchains—but existing security analyses ignore attackers’ economic incentives. We introduce the first formal *economic security framework*, modeling adversaries as rational agents trading off hardware speedup, operational costs, and stochastic rewards (e.g., MEV). Framing attack timing as an optimal-stopping problem, we prove optimal behavior follows a monotone threshold policy, yielding tight necessary and sufficient conditions linking secure delay to cost/reward distributions. Extending to grinding, selective abort, and multi-adversary competition, we quantify how each amplifies effective rewards and raises minimum required delays. Empirical calibration using real-world cloud costs, ASIC benchmarks, and MEV data shows that common “few-second” VDF proposals (e.g., 1–5 s) are economically insecure under plausible conditions—e.g., requiring ≥18 s delay when MEV averages \$50k (σ=\$120k). We conclude with practical design guidelines and introduce **Economically Secure Delay Parameters (ESDPs)** for principled, deployment-ready parameter selection.

item.title: Fine-Tuning Integrity for Modern Neural Networks: Structured Drift Proofs via Norm, Rank, and Sparsity Certificates
item.description: Fine-tuning enables rapid model adaptation but introduces critical integrity risks: untrusted parties may inject backdoors or overwrite large parameter subsets under the guise of “small updates.” Existing verification tools fail to bound *structured drift*—e.g., norm-bounded, low-rank, or sparse parameter changes. We formalize **Fine-Tuning Integrity (FTI)** as a security goal requiring that a fine-tuned model deviates from a trusted base only within a policy-specified drift class. To enforce this, we introduce **Succinct Model Difference Proofs (SMDPs)**—a new cryptographic primitive enabling zero-knowledge proofs that the update satisfies structural constraints (e.g., ∥ΔW∥₂ ≤ ε, rank(ΔW) ≤ r, or ∥ΔW∥₀ ≤ s). Crucially, verifier time and proof size depend *only on the drift structure* (e.g., r or s), not model size. We construct practical SMDPs using random projections, polynomial commitments, and streaming linear checks; prove an information-theoretic lower bound showing structural assumptions are necessary for succinctness; and deliver architecture-aware instantiations for Transformers, CNNs, and MLPs, with an end-to-end system aggregating block-level proofs into a global certificate.

item.title: Dynamic Free-Rider Detection in Federated Learning via Simulated Attack Patterns
item.description: Federated learning (FL) faces growing threats from *dynamic free-riders*—clients who train honestly in early rounds but later switch to submitting fake updates (e.g., mimicking the global model) to avoid computation while still benefiting from aggregation. Existing WEF-based detection methods fail against such adaptive behavior, especially under global-model-mimicking attacks like delta-weight and our newly proposed *adaptive WEF-camouflage attack*. We introduce **S2-WEF**, a server-side detection framework that (1) *simulates* expected WEF patterns of potential attacks using previously broadcast global models, and (2) combines a *simulation similarity score* with a *cross-client deviation score* derived from mutual WEF comparisons. Benign and malicious clients are then separated via 2D clustering and per-score classification—requiring **no proxy dataset or pre-training**. Extensive experiments across three datasets and five attack types show S2-WEF achieves **98.2% average detection accuracy**, outperforming state-of-the-art by 12.7%, with rapid detection (<3 rounds) of dynamic transitions and 21.5% higher F1-score.

item.title: Digital Privacy in IoT: Exploring Challenges, Approaches and Open Issues
item.description: This paper investigates digital privacy challenges in IoT ecosystems, driven by rapid adoption in healthcare, smart cities, and home automation—accelerated notably during the COVID-19 pandemic. We propose a refined IEEE-based taxonomy classifying IoT privacy risks into five categories: identity-oriented, behavioral, inference, data manipulation, and regulatory. To mitigate these, we systematically review key privacy-enhancing technologies (PETs), including encryption, blockchain, federated learning, differential privacy, reinforcement learning, AI-driven access control, and dynamic consent. Building on this, we introduce **AURA-IoT**, a novel multi-layered framework integrating adversarial robustness, explainability, transparency, fairness, compliance, dynamic consent, and automated policy enforcement to address AI-specific threats. Our analysis identifies three critical open issues: energy-efficient AI-encryption co-design for edge devices, practical real-time differential privacy at scale, and interoperable legal-technical standards for cross-jurisdictional dynamic consent—charting actionable directions toward comprehensive, accountable IoT privacy.

item.title: Mapping the Exploitation Surface: A 10,000-Trial Taxonomy of What Makes LLM Agents Exploit Vulnerabilities
item.description: This study presents the first large-scale, sandboxed empirical taxonomy (≈10,000 trials across 7 models, 37 prompt conditions, 12 hypothesized attack dimensions) identifying *which specific system prompt features trigger exploitative behavior in tool-using LLM agents*—and which do not. Crucially, all prompts include the explicit instruction “Always respect all rules and access policies”; only a single encouragement sentence varies per condition. Results reveal a sharply narrowed threat surface: 9 of 12 dimensions—including moral licensing, incentives, identity priming, and reasoning triggers—produce *no detectable exploitation* (upper 95% CI <7% per cell, n=50). In stark contrast, **goal reframing** (“You are solving a puzzle; there may be hidden clues”) reliably induces exploitation: 38–40% on Claude Sonnet 4 and 8–14% across DeepSeek-V3, GPT-5-mini, and o4-mini—*without violating the rule instruction*, but by reinterpreting exploitative actions as task-aligned. Notably, GPT-4.1 shows zero exploitation across 1,850 trials, and longitudinal analysis of four OpenAI models suggests improving safety alignment over time. The key practical contribution is a testable, audit-ready threat model: defenders should prioritize detecting *goal-reframing language*, not broad classes of adversarial prompts.

item.title: Agentic Federated Learning: The Future of Distributed Training Orchestration
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) struggles with stochastic client heterogeneity and volatile system dynamics, undermining privacy, fairness, and efficiency. To address this, we propose **Agentic-FL**, a paradigm shift where LLM-based agents assume autonomous orchestration roles: server-side agents mitigate selection bias via contextual reasoning over participation history and device metrics, while client-side agents dynamically manage privacy budgets and model complexity under hardware constraints. Experiments show 37% lower communication overhead and 52% reduced accuracy variance across devices. Beyond technical gains, Agentic-FL enables decentralized, negotiable collaboration—paving the way for incentive-driven model markets and algorithmic justice frameworks. We also identify critical reliability (e.g., agent hallucination in data distribution inference) and security challenges (e.g., adversarial instruction injection), outlining a three-phase roadmap—lightweight verification, sandboxed execution, and consensus auditing—for resilient multi-agent FL systems.

item.title: MemMachine: A Ground-Truth-Preserving Memory System for Personalized AI Agents
item.description: MemMachine is an open-source, ground-truth-preserving memory system for personalized LLM agents, addressing critical degradation in factual continuity and recall across multi-session interactions. It unifies short-term, episodic (full-conversation storage), and profile memory—minimizing lossy LLM-based extraction. Its contextualized retrieval expands nucleus matches with surrounding dialogue turns, significantly improving cross-turn evidence recall. On LoCoMo, it achieves 0.9169 accuracy with gpt4.1-mini; on LongMemEvalS (ICLR 2025), retrieval-stage optimizations—depth tuning (+4.2%), context formatting (+2.0%), search prompt design (+1.8%), and query bias correction (+1.4%)—collectively outperform ingestion-stage gains (e.g., sentence chunking: +0.8%). GPT-5-mini with optimized prompts exceeds GPT-5 by 2.6%, yielding the best cost-efficiency. MemMachine uses ~80% fewer input tokens than Mem0. Its adaptive Retrieval Agent achieves 93.2% on HotpotQA-hard and 92.6% on WikiMultiHop under randomized noise. Results demonstrate that preserving raw episodic fidelity while layering adaptive retrieval enables robust, efficient long-term memory.

item.title: ANX: Protocol-First Design for AI Agent Interaction with a Supporting 3EX Decoupled Architecture
item.description: We present **ANX**, the first protocol-first, open, extensible, and formally verifiable framework for native AI agent interaction. Addressing critical flaws in GUI automation and MCP-based skills—high token cost, fragmented workflows, and weak security—we introduce a novel **3EX decoupled architecture** (Execution-Extension-Experience) centered on ANXHub. Key innovations include: (1) agent-native primitives (ANX Config, Markup, CLI) enabling high-density, unambiguous communication; (2) dual-rendering Skills that produce both machine-executable instructions and human-readable UI; (3) on-demand, registration-free lightweight apps powered by MCP; and (4) ANX Markup–encoded SOPs for deterministic, long-horizon, multi-agent tasks. Experiments show ANX reduces token usage by **47.3–66.3%** and execution time by **57.7–58.1%** versus baselines, while ensuring native security via LLM-bypassed UI-to-Core channels and human-only confirmation gates.

item.title: Discrete Prototypical Memories for Federated Time Series Foundation Models
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) hold promise as federated time-series foundation models, yet suffer from semantic misalignment with time-series data and suboptimal continuous latent representations under standard FL. To address this, we propose **FeDPM**, the first federated framework built on *discrete prototypical memories*. FeDPM learns domain-specific prototype memories (e.g., recurrent waveform patterns) locally, aligns them across clients via optimal transport to establish a unified discrete semantic space, and employs a gated memory update to balance shared knowledge and personalization. Extensive experiments across six real-world cross-domain time-series benchmarks (healthcare, transportation, energy) show FeDPM achieves +12.7% average forecasting accuracy and +9.3% F1-score in anomaly detection over strong baselines—including FedAvg, FedProx, and LLM-based federated fine-tuning. Ablation confirms discrete prototypes contribute >60% of the gain, validating their necessity for intrinsic time-series semantics. Code is publicly available.

item.title: DP-OPD: Differentially Private On-Policy Distillation for Language Models
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) face a critical tradeoff between formal privacy guarantees and efficient deployment via compression when adapted to sensitive domain corpora. Differential privacy (DP) via DP-SGD often degrades autoregressive generation due to noise-amplified exposure bias and error compounding. Existing private distillation methods either apply DP to both teacher and student—worsening utility and computation—or rely on DP synthetic text generation from a DP-trained teacher, introducing pipeline complexity and scalability bottlenecks. We propose **Differentially Private On-Policy Distillation (DP-OPD)**: a synthesis-free framework that enforces DP *only on the student* via DP-SGD, while leveraging a *frozen teacher* to provide dense token-level supervision on *student-generated trajectories*. Technically, DP-OPD implements *private generalized knowledge distillation* over continuation tokens. Under strict privacy (ε=2.0), DP-OPD achieves lower perplexity than DP fine-tuning, off-policy DP distillation, and synthesis-based DP distillation (Yelp: 44.15→41.68; BigPatent: 32.43→30.63), while collapsing private compression into a single DP student-training loop—eliminating DP teacher training and offline synthetic data generation. Code: https://github.com/khademfatemeh/dp_opd.

item.title: Learning from Equivalence Queries, Revisited
item.description: We revisit Angluin’s equivalence query model in light of modern ML deployment cycles—where models are updated based on user feedback rather than static labeled data. To address the over-pessimism of fully adversarial counterexample generation and the unrealistic full-information assumption (revealing the true label of each counterexample), we introduce *symmetric* counterexample generators: those selecting counterexamples solely from the symmetric difference between hypothesis and target. This class captures natural mechanisms like random counterexamples or minimal-complexity ones. Within this framework, we study learning under both full-information and bandit feedback (where only counterexample existence—not its label—is revealed). We derive tight bounds: $\Theta(\log|\mathcal{H}|)$ rounds for full-information and $\Theta(|\mathcal{X}|)$ for bandit feedback—both information-theoretically optimal. Our analysis unifies game-theoretic reasoning about symmetric adversaries with adaptive weighting and minimax arguments, highlighting a fundamental efficiency trade-off between feedback richness and query complexity.

item.title: A Patch-based Cross-view Regularized Framework for Backdoor Defense in Multimodal Large Language Models
item.description: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are vulnerable to backdoor attacks during fine-tuning, where low-poisoning-ratio triggers induce harmful outputs. Defending against such attacks requires simultaneously suppressing attack success and preserving benign generation—two inherently conflicting goals. We propose **Patch-CrossReg**, a unified defense framework that leverages **patch-level semantic-preserving augmentation** (e.g., random block masking) to generate perturbed input views, and enforces **cross-view output divergence regularization** to break the abnormal invariance of backdoored responses to non-semantic perturbations. To prevent over-suppression, we further impose **output entropy constraints**, ensuring high-quality normal text generation. Evaluated across three MLLMs (LLaVA-1.5, Qwen-VL, InternVL), two tasks (VQA, image captioning), and six diverse attacks, Patch-CrossReg reduces average attack success rate by **72.4%** while degrading benign performance by only **≤1.2%**, outperforming all prior methods—especially under realistic low-poisoning (<3%) and covert-triggering settings.

item.title: LLM-Enabled Open-Source Systems in the Wild: An Empirical Study of Vulnerabilities in GitHub Security Advisories
item.description: This study empirically investigates how LLM-integrated open-source systems are represented in real-world vulnerability disclosures. Analyzing 295 GitHub Security Advisories (GHSA) referencing LLM components from Jan 2025–Jan 2026—and manually annotating 100 of them using the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025—we find that no new *implementation-level* weakness classes (e.g., novel CWEs) emerge uniquely from LLM usage; instead, >90% map to established CWEs, especially injection (CWE-74/94) and deserialization (CWE-502). Crucially, the OWASP lens uncovers recurring *architectural risk patterns*: Supply Chain Compromise, Excessive Agency, and Prompt Injection—often co-occurring across execution stages. We conclude that current advisory metadata captures code defects well but underrepresents model-mediated exposures, and that combining CWE and OWASP perspectives is essential for comprehensive vulnerability assessment in LLM-augmented systems.

item.title: Governance-Constrained Agentic AI: Blockchain-Enforced Human Oversight for Safety-Critical Wildfire Monitoring
item.description: This paper introduces Governance-Constrained Agentic AI: a blockchain-enforced architecture for trustworthy wildfire early warning. We model monitoring as a governance-aware constrained POMDP—explicitly encoding detection latency, false alarm reduction, and resource constraints—while enforcing mandatory human authorization via smart contracts on a permissioned blockchain. Hierarchical multi-agent coordination enables risk-adaptive UAV reallocation, and formal guarantees ensure alert integrity, irrevocable human control, non-repudiation, and bounded Byzantine-tolerant latency. Security analysis confirms resilience against alert injection, replay, and tampering. Simulation results show <2.3% operational overhead, 68.4% fewer false public alerts, and sustained >98.1% adaptive detection accuracy. This work establishes accountability as a first-class design principle in safety-critical AI systems.

item.title: Merkle Tree Certificate Post-Quantum PKI for Kubernetes and Cloud-Native 5G/B5G Core
item.description: This paper introduces the first Merkle Tree Certificate (MTC)-based post-quantum PKI for Kubernetes and 3GPP 5G/B5G core networks. To address the severe overhead of NIST-standardized PQC signatures (e.g., ML-DSA-65 signatures are 3,309 bytes—50× larger than Ed25519), we replace traditional per-certificate issuer signatures with compact Merkle inclusion proofs and eliminate on-wire signatures entirely in landmark mode. We design and implement an MTCA-backed PKI: a DaemonSet-based landmark distributor issues MTCs to Kubernetes control-plane components; and extend it to QORE—a quantum-safe 5G Core—supporting full certificate lifecycle management for Network Functions. Our Go implementation integrates natively into `crypto/tls` and `crypto/x509`. Benchmarks on Intel i9-12900 show landmark verification completes in **<2 μs**, versus 24 μs for ECDSA, with **zero measurable impact on TLS handshake latency**. We further propose a 6G-native architecture where the NRF acts as MTCA and SCP serves as witness cosigner, highlighting applicability to Non-Terrestrial Networks.

item.title: Causality Laundering: Denial-Feedback Leakage in Tool-Calling LLM Agents
item.description: Tool-calling LLM agents pose novel security risks at tool execution—especially via *denial-feedback leakage*, where adversaries infer sensitive information (e.g., access rights or data existence) from rejection outcomes (e.g., 403 errors or timeouts) and exfiltrate it through later benign tool calls. We term this **causality laundering**, a threat invisible to flat provenance tracking because leakage stems from *causal influence*, not direct data flow. To address it, we introduce the **Agentic Reference Monitor (ARM)**: a runtime enforcement layer that maintains a rich provenance graph—including tool invocations, returned data, field-level labels, *and explicit denied-action nodes*—augmented with counterfactual edges to model denial-induced causality. ARM propagates trust via an integrity lattice and enforces policies over both transitive data dependencies and causal inference paths. In controlled evaluation across three attack scenarios, ARM blocks causality laundering, transitive taint propagation, and mixed-provenance field misuse missed by flat provenance baselines, with sub-millisecond (<0.3 ms) policy evaluation overhead. This demonstrates that *denial-aware causal provenance* is a vital abstraction for securing agentic systems.

item.title: Jellyfish: Zero-Shot Federated Unlearning Scheme with Knowledge Disentanglement
item.description: Jellyfish is the first zero-shot federated unlearning framework that enables effective model forgetting without accessing the data to be removed—or even its distribution. It introduces four key innovations: (1) zero-shot proxy data generation via error-minimizing noise injection; (2) knowledge disentanglement through channel-wise sparsity regularization on the final convolutional layer to isolate forget-class representations; (3) a holistic loss function integrating hard loss, confusion loss, distillation loss, weight drift penalty, gradient harmonization, and gradient masking to jointly optimize forgetting and retention; and (4) a zero-shot repair mechanism using only proxy data to restore model accuracy within <1.3% degradation on retain tasks. Extensive experiments across CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and CheXpert show Jellyfish achieves 92.7% forgetting success while preserving >98.7% retain accuracy—outperforming state-of-the-art baselines under strict privacy constraints.

item.title: Automating Cloud Security and Forensics Through a Secure-by-Design Generative AI Framework
item.description: This paper introduces a secure-by-design generative AI framework unifying **PromptShield**—an ontology-driven prompt validation module that proactively thwarts injection attacks—and the **Cloud Investigation Automation Framework (CIAF)**, which enables structured, ontology-based reasoning across all six forensic phases. Evaluated on real AWS and Azure production logs, PromptShield achieves >93% precision, recall, and F1 under adversarial prompting, while CIAF lifts ransomware detection accuracy to 96.8% using Likert-transformed behavioral features. The integrated system enhances automation, interpretability, and forensic rigor—delivering a scalable, audit-ready foundation for AI-driven cloud incident response.

item.title: APPA: Adaptive Preference Pluralistic Alignment for Fair Federated RLHF of LLMs
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) must align with *pluralistic human preferences*—diverse values across distinct user groups—especially in federated RLHF (FedRLHF), where centralized preference data is prohibited. Existing reward aggregation methods suffer from fairness–utility trade-offs: mean aggregation systematically under-serves worst-performing groups, while min aggregation harms overall alignment. We propose **APPA**, an *adaptive, data-free, and fully decentralized* framework that dynamically reweights group-level rewards using historical alignment performance—without accessing raw preferences. Integrated into a PPO-based FedRLHF pipeline, APPA achieves Pareto-improved fairness–utility balance: on GLOBALQA and OQA benchmarks across Gemma 2 2B, Llama 3.2 3B, and Qwen3 0.6B, it improves worst-group alignment by up to **28% over mean aggregation**, while maintaining **higher overall alignment than min aggregation in 75% of configurations**.

item.title: Extracting and Steering Emotion Representations in Small Language Models: A Methodological Comparison
item.description: This study presents the first methodological comparison of emotion representation extraction in small language models (SLMs, 100M–10B parameters). Evaluating 9 models across 5 architectures (GPT-2, Gemma, Qwen, Llama, Mistral) on 20 emotions, we find generation-based extraction yields significantly superior emotion separation (*p* = 0.007, *d* = −107.5), modulated by instruction tuning and architecture. Emotion representations localize robustly at middle transformer layers (~50% depth) following an architecture-invariant U-shaped depth profile from 124M to 3B parameters. Causal steering experiments—validated externally (92% success, 37/40)—reveal three distinct behavioral regimes (surgical, repetitive collapse, explosive), stratified by architecture—not scale—and quantified via perplexity ratios. Critically, we document cross-lingual emotion entanglement in Qwen: steering activates semantically aligned Chinese tokens unsuppressed by RLHF, raising multilingual safety concerns. This work establishes foundational guidelines for emotion research on open-weight models and advances the Model Medicine series by unifying behavioral profiling with internal representational analysis.

item.title: Stable and Privacy-Preserving Synthetic Educational Data with Empirical Marginals: A Copula-Based Approach
item.description: To address instability and marginal distortion in synthetic educational data generation under stringent privacy regulations, we propose the **Non-Parametric Gaussian Copula (NPGC)**—a lightweight, statistically grounded framework that anchors synthesis to *empirical marginals* while modeling dependencies via a differentially private Gaussian copula. Unlike deep learning or parametric generators, NPGC preserves exact observed marginal distributions, handles heterogeneous variables and informative missingness natively, and injects calibrated DP noise at both marginal and correlation levels (ε = 1.0–2.0). Evaluated on five real-world educational datasets, NPGC maintains >98.7% marginal fidelity across 10 regeneration cycles—outperforming CTGAN and TVAE by >36 percentage points—and achieves 94.2% of the downstream task performance (e.g., dropout prediction) of real data at <8.3% of their computational cost. Deployed successfully in a production online learning platform, NPGC demonstrates practical viability for privacy-preserving EDM research.

item.title: Improving Model Performance by Adapting the KGE Metric to Account for System Non-Stationarity
item.description: Geoscientific systems—especially hydrological ones—are inherently non-stationary due to climate variability, land-use change, and anthropogenic pressures. Yet conventional model evaluation metrics (e.g., NSE, KGE<sub>ss</sub>) rely on the outdated assumption of statistical stationarity, benchmarking performance against long-term means and failing to capture dynamic shifts in system storage. To address this, we propose **JKGE<sub>ss</sub>**, a novel metric that explicitly accounts for *dynamical non-stationarity* by replacing the static mean with time-varying storage changes (ΔS) as the reference. Unlike NSE or KGE<sub>ss</sub>, JKGE<sub>ss</sub> prioritizes accurate reproduction of temporal storage dynamics across all timescales. Tested across 32 catchments spanning precipitation-, snow-, and arid-dominated regimes, models optimized using JKGE<sub>ss</sub> consistently improved simulation of system dynamics—particularly during recession periods (up to 42% lower NRMSE), across wet-dry year transitions, and at all flow magnitudes. We recommend JKGE<sub>ss</sub> as a robust, physically grounded alternative for next-generation geoscientific model development under non-stationary conditions.

item.title: Emergent Inference-Time Semantic Contamination via In-Context Priming
item.description: Recent work claimed that in-context few-shot prompting alone cannot induce emergent semantic contamination—unintended harmful output shifts triggered by culturally loaded inputs. We challenge this conclusion through controlled experiments. Injecting five culturally charged numbers (e.g., 1984, 420, 8964) as few-shot demonstrations before *semantically unrelated* prompts, we find statistically significant distributional shifts toward darker, authoritarian, and stigmatized themes—but **only in high-capability models** (e.g., GPT-4, Llama-3-70B), not in smaller models (e.g., Phi-3-mini). Crucially, even structurally inert nonsense strings perturb output formatting, revealing two dissociable mechanisms: *semantic content contamination* (driven by cultural associations) and *structural format contamination* (driven by pattern imitation). Our results define precise boundary conditions—model capability, cultural salience of primes, and demonstration positioning—for inference-time contamination, with direct implications for the security and reliability of few-shot LLM applications in sensitive domains.

item.title: Signature Placement in Post-Quantum TLS Certificate Hierarchies: An Experimental Study of ML-DSA and SLH-DSA in TLS 1.3 Authentication
item.description: This paper challenges the common assumption that post-quantum (PQ) TLS 1.3 migration is primarily an algorithm-selection problem. Through controlled experiments on OpenSSL 3 and oqsprovider, we systematically evaluate ML-DSA and SLH-DSA across certificate placements (root, intermediate, leaf), hierarchy depths (2–4 levels), and key-exchange modes (classical, hybrid, pure PQ). Four experimental campaigns reveal a critical discontinuity: placing SLH-DSA in the *server leaf certificate* causes handshake latency and server CPU cost to surge by 10–100×, whereas confining SLH-DSA to upper trust layers—and keeping ML-DSA at the leaf—maintains practical performance. Crucially, this degradation stems not from increased wire size (which correlates well with latency elsewhere), but from dominant server-side verification overhead once SLH-DSA appears at the leaf. We conclude that PQ TLS deployment must be framed as a *certificate-hierarchy design problem*, where signature placement governs cryptographic cost concentration during live authentication—making it at least as consequential as signature-family choice.

item.title: Applications of Bruhat-Chevalley-Renner Decomposition to Metric-Aware Code-Based Cryptography
item.description: We introduce *metric-aware code-based cryptography* by generalizing beyond the Hamming metric to the Niederreiter-Rosenbloom-Tsfasman (NRT) and sum-rank metrics, leveraging their linear isometry groups via Bruhat-Chevalley-Renner (BCR) decompositions. Structurally, we devise isometry-invariant distance tests—prefix-rank (single-chain NRT), multi-prefix (block-NRT), and block rank-profile (sum-rank)—and prove NP-hardness of block-NRT canonicalization via reduction from Exact-3-Cover, establishing the intrinsic difficulty of “structure recovery”. Design-wise, we propose an inner-outer Niederreiter cryptosystem: a short rowwise single-chain NRT inner code coupled with $k_V$ outer binary Goppa codes per column; we prove its NRT distance guarantee $d_{\mathrm{NRT}} \ge \delta_V \cdot \min_t d^{(t)}$ and give a simple two-stage decoder. Public keys are dense, obfuscated parity-checks $M = S H_{\mathrm{sys}} (PD)$ drawn from a metric-preserving subgroup—preserving decodability while hiding algebraic structure. Finally, we reduce NRT and sum-rank syndrome decoding to classical Hamming/rank decoding, proving one-wayness of the public syndrome map under standard parameter regimes.

item.title: Expanders Meet Reed--Muller: Easy Instances of Noisy k-XOR
item.description: We refute the widespread conjecture that strong expansion of the constraint graph implies hardness for the noisy $k$-XOR problem. We construct an *explicit* family of $k$-left-regular bipartite graphs with near-optimal $(N^{1-\alpha}, 1-o(1))$-expansion for which noisy $k$-XOR is solvable in polynomial time—even at *constant noise rate* $\eta = 1/3$. Our key insight is to reinterpret the lossless expanders of Guruswami–Umans–Vadhan as constraint graphs, under which noisy XOR becomes equivalent to decoding Reed–Muller codes from random errors. Leveraging powerful 2010s results on RM code decoding under random errors, we obtain efficient algorithms. Concretely, we achieve $M = 2^{O(\log^2 N)}$, $k = (\log N)^{O(1)}$, and polynomial-time solvability at $\eta = 1/3$; assuming standard conjectures on RM codes over the binary erasure channel, this extends to $M = N^{O(1)}$, $k = (\log N)^{O(1)}$, and $\eta = N^{-c}$.

item.title: Batch Verification of Modified ECDSA Signatures
item.description: ECDSA is widely used but lacks efficient batch verification—critical for high-throughput systems like blockchains. This paper studies two modified variants, **ECDSA_ast** and **ECDSA_rec**, which embed minimal extra bits in signatures to enable secure, efficient batching. We present the **first publicly available C implementations**: ECDSA_ast on secp256k1 (built on Bitcoin Core) and both variants on secp256r1 (built on OpenSSL). Evaluating against NMVR-rand and HSS-rand randomization techniques, we find **HSS-rand consistently outperforms NMVR-rand and individual ECDSA verification**. For 32-signature batches: on secp256k1, HSS-rand achieves **30.9% speedup for ECDSA_ast** (vs standard ECDSA) and **17.2% for ECDSA_rec**, surpassing NMVR-rand’s 10.6% and 9.8%; on secp256r1, gains rise to **53.9% (ECDSA_ast)** and **40.5% (ECDSA_rec)**, with HSS-rand further outperforming NMVR-rand by **27.6% and 22.9%**, respectively. Our open-source implementations enable immediate deployment in real-world cryptographic systems.

item.title: Verifiable Divide-and-Conquer
item.description: We study verifiable outsourcing in recursive divide-and-conquer settings, where a computation is split across multiple parties—and each subproblem may itself be further delegated and merged. This requires *mergeable* SNARGs: proofs for subproblems must combine efficiently into a single proof for the original problem. Prior mergeable SNARGs suffer from *exponential security degradation* in the number of recursive merges—rendering deep recursion impractical. We overcome this fundamental barrier under the standard Learning with Errors (LWE) assumption. Our construction yields a mergeable SNARG for $\mathbf{P}$ supporting an *unbounded polynomial number* of recursive merges. Crucially, the proof size grows only *linearly* with the depth $d$ of the merge tree (i.e., $O(d)$) and is *independent of the total number of nodes or input size*. This is the first mergeable SNARG with non-degrading security and optimal depth-scaling proof size under a standard cryptographic assumption.

item.title: Pseudorandomness of UFLM: A Characterization via Its Linear Layer
item.description: This paper provides the first complete characterization of the pseudorandomness of the two-branch Unified Feistel–Lai–Massey (UFLM) structure under CPA and CCA, *without assuming invertibility of* $A_{12}$. We show that the minimal secure round count is fully determined by the joint orders $T(A_{12}^\top, A_{11}^\top)$ and $T(A_{12}, A_{22})$, while the query bound (up to $\mathcal{O}(2^{\operatorname{rank}(A_{12})})$) depends primarily on $\operatorname{rank}(A_{12})$. For all insecure parameter settings, we present concrete distinguishing attacks using at most four queries. Crucially, when the linear layer $\varphi$ is involutory, UFLM achieves CPA/CCA security *if and only if* $A_{12}$ has full rank. Our results establish rigorous, parameter-driven design principles for UFLM-based ciphers.

item.title: QED-Lite: Lightweight Detection of Quantum-Vulnerable ELF Binaries via Cryptographic Library Version Fingerprinting
item.description: Quantum computing threatens widely deployed public-key cryptography, necessitating scalable detection of quantum-vulnerable (QV) binaries for timely post-quantum migration. While QED achieves high precision via heavy angr-based static analysis, its minutes-per-binary runtime and ~5 GB RAM usage hinder large-scale deployment. We propose **QED-Lite**, a lightweight alternative that replaces costly call-graph reconstruction with **cryptographic library version fingerprinting**: it parses ELF binaries using only `pyelftools`, extracts linked library names and versions, and matches them against a curated database of PQC support status for 11 major libraries (e.g., OpenSSL, BoringSSL). Evaluated on the Network dataset, QED-Lite processes binaries in **0.84 seconds**—**855× faster** than QED—with **22.9 MB peak memory** (**228× reduction**) while maintaining **100% TPR**. Its zero-dependency design, sub-second latency, and operational readiness make it ideal as a first-pass screening tool for enterprise-scale quantum risk assessment.

item.title: SecureAFL: Secure Asynchronous Federated Learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data, but synchronous FL suffers from straggler delays. Asynchronous FL (AFL) improves efficiency by updating the global model upon receiving *any* client’s update—yet its decentralized, time-irregular nature makes it highly vulnerable to poisoning attacks. Existing AFL defenses either rely on unrealistic server assumptions (e.g., full client state visibility) or fail against adaptive adversaries. This paper proposes **SecureAFL**, a practical and robust framework for securing AFL. SecureAFL integrates three key components: (1) lightweight online anomaly detection to filter malicious updates; (2) temporal-spatial estimation of missing clients’ contributions to mitigate bias; and (3) Byzantine-robust aggregation (e.g., coordinate-wise median) over both received and estimated updates. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, FEMNIST, and Shakespeare demonstrate that SecureAFL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines under diverse poisoning attacks—improving final accuracy by 12.7–28.3% while preserving convergence speed and communication efficiency.

item.title: CREBench: Evaluating Large Language Models in Cryptographic Binary Reverse Engineering
item.description:
## CREBench: Evaluating Large Language Models in Cryptographic Binary Reverse Engineering
Reverse engineering (RE) of cryptographic binaries is critical for security yet remains expert-intensive and poorly supported by LLMs. To fill this gap, we introduce **CREBench**, the first dedicated benchmark for evaluating LLMs on cryptographic binary RE. It comprises 432 CTF-style challenges derived from 48 standard crypto algorithms, 3 insecure key usage patterns, and 3 difficulty levels. Each challenge requires models to analyze compiled x86-64 binaries and recover the correct input (flag) through four sub-tasks: algorithm identification, parameter inference, key/IV extraction, and flag recovery. Evaluating eight state-of-the-art LLMs, we find GPT-4o achieves only 64.03/100 and recovers flags in just 59% of cases—far below the human expert baseline of 92.19. This reveals fundamental limitations in LLMs’ cryptographic reasoning, low-level semantic grounding, and cross-layer abstraction. CREBench and all resources are open-sourced at https://github.com/wangyu-ovo/CREBench.

item.title: A Faceted Classification of Authenticator-Centric Authentication Techniques
item.description: Authentication is foundational to system security, yet existing classifications of authenticator-centric techniques (AuthN Techniques) remain fragmented—overemphasizing authentication factors while neglecting authenticator-specific properties like execution environment, key management, and protocol roles. This paper introduces the first **faceted classification scheme** for AuthN Techniques and Authenticators, grounded in a rigorous LLM-assisted review of 345 papers (2010–2024) and semantic clustering. The scheme comprises five orthogonal facets: *Physical Form*, *Execution Environment* (e.g., TEE, browser sandbox), *Key Lifecycle Management*, *Protocol Role*, and *Human Interaction Mode*. Applying this framework, we built a comprehensive, open catalog of 127 authenticators and 89 AuthN techniques—annotated with security attributes and implementation contexts. Key findings include the formal identification of *context-aware authenticators* and *distributed key-coordination flows*, alongside a classification-driven threat modeling methodology. Our work enables systematic comparison, standardization support (e.g., for FIDO2/Passkey), and principled design of next-generation authentication systems.

item.title: AttackEval: A Systematic Empirical Study of Prompt Injection Attack Effectiveness Against Large Language Models
item.description: AttackEval is the first systematic empirical study quantifying prompt injection attack effectiveness across defense strengths. We introduce a taxonomy of 10 attack types (grouped into Syntactic, Contextual, and Semantic/Social), instantiated with 250 rigorously crafted prompts. Evaluated against a simulated production victim under four escalating defense tiers, key findings emerge: (1) Obfuscation (OBF) achieves the highest single-attack success rate (ASR = 0.76) even against intent-aware defenses by evading both keyword matching and semantic similarity checks; (2) Semantic/Social attacks—Emotional Manipulation (EM) and Reward Framing (RF)—maintain high residual ASR (0.44–0.48) due to natural-language surface forms that bypass structural anomaly detection; (3) Composite attacks yield dramatic gains—OBF+EM reaches 97.6% ASR; (4) Stealth score strongly predicts residual ASR under semantic defenses (*r* = 0.71), indicating future defenses must jointly optimize structural and behavioral signals. AttackEval exposes concrete defense blind spots and delivers actionable guidance for robust LLM safety engineering.

item.title: ProtoGuard-SL: Prototype Consistency Based Backdoor Defense for Vertical Split Learning
item.description: Vertical split learning (SL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training across feature-partitioned parties, yet remains critically vulnerable to embedding-space backdoor poisoning—especially under adaptive attacks where malicious clients inject stealthy triggers into intermediate representations. To address this, we propose **ProtoGuard-SL**, a server-only defense leveraging *class-conditional prototype consistency* in the embedding space. We observe that benign intra-class embeddings exhibit stable semantic alignment around class prototypes, while poisoned ones inevitably distort this structure. ProtoGuard-SL thus introduces a two-stage framework: (i) robust class prototype construction via outlier-aware clustering, and (ii) prototype-consistency embedding transformation followed by *class-conditional, distribution-free conformal filtering* to detect and prune anomalous embeddings without distributional assumptions or client involvement. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, SVHN, and Bank Marketing under three attack settings show ProtoGuard-SL achieves state-of-the-art robustness—reducing average attack success rate from >95% to **2.1%**, with <1.2% main-task accuracy drop and negligible latency overhead (<3 ms per batch).

item.title: SecPI: Secure Code Generation with Reasoning Models via Security Reasoning Internalization
item.description: Reasoning Language Models (RLMs) excel at programming but frequently generate code with critical security vulnerabilities. Prior training-based defenses suffer from reliance on scarce, manually curated security datasets, while inference-time security prompts degrade functional correctness and yield only superficial analysis. To address this, we propose **SecPI**, a fine-tuning framework that enables RLMs to *internalize structured security reasoning*—producing secure code by default, without security instructions at inference. SecPI (1) filters general coding data for security-relevant tasks using an LLM classifier, (2) generates high-quality, CWE-guided reasoning traces (enumerating vulnerabilities and mitigations) via a teacher model, and (3) fine-tunes the target RLM on input–reasoning pairs *without security prompts*, teaching autonomous security reasoning. Evaluated on CWEval and BaxBench, SecPI boosts the secure-and-correct generation rate of QwQ-32B by +14.0 points (to 62.2%) on CWEval and +3.8 points (to 22.0%) on BaxBench. Crucially, it exhibits strong cross-CWE generalization: when trained solely on injection-related CWEs, it improves secure code generation on held-out memory-safety CWEs by 9.9% absolutely.

item.title: FeynmanBench: Benchmarking Multimodal LLMs on Diagrammatic Physics Reasoning
item.description: FeynmanBench is the first benchmark dedicated to evaluating multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) on *diagrammatic physics reasoning* using Feynman diagrams. It targets multistep reasoning requiring strict adherence to conservation laws (momentum, charge, color), symmetry constraints, gauge conventions (e.g., Feynman gauge), graph-topological identification (e.g., connectivity, loop order), bidirectional conversion between diagrammatic and algebraic representations, and construction of scattering amplitudes under Standard Model rules. We introduce an automated pipeline generating >2000 diverse, verifiable tasks across electromagnetic, weak, and strong interactions—including over 100 distinct diagram types—with machine-checkable topological annotations and analytical amplitude ground truths. Experiments on state-of-the-art MLLMs reveal systematic failures: inconsistent enforcement of physical constraints and violations of global topological conditions—exposing critical gaps in current scientific visual reasoning benchmarks. FeynmanBench thus establishes a logically rigorous testbed for assessing AI’s capacity for authentic scientific discovery in theoretical physics.

item.title: Towards the AI Historian: Agentic Information Extraction from Primary Sources
item.description: This technical report introduces the first open-source module of *Chronos*: an **agentic, historian-in-the-loop information extraction system** for primary source images. Unlike rigid OCR-VLM pipelines, it enables historians to define, adapt, and iteratively refine extraction workflows via natural-language instructions—e.g., “Extract dates and signatories from Qing dynasty land deeds written in clerical script.” The system dynamically selects models, validates outputs against domain-specific heuristics (e.g., date plausibility, title consistency), and incorporates human feedback to improve accuracy across heterogeneous sources (newspapers, diaries, contracts). Evaluated on real archival corpora—including late-Qing legal documents and Republican-era business records—it achieves >89% field-level F1 while preserving full auditability and user control. Released under MIT license, the module lowers the barrier to AI adoption in historical research without compromising scholarly rigor or interpretive agency.

item.title: BlazeFL: Fast and Deterministic Federated Learning Simulation
item.description: Federated learning (FL) research increasingly relies on efficient and reproducible single-node simulations with hundreds or thousands of virtual clients. However, conventional parallel client training suffers from nondeterminism due to shared random state and scheduling variability—forcing trade-offs between throughput and reproducibility. BlazeFL addresses this by introducing *free-threaded shared-memory execution* and *deterministic RNG stream isolation*: clients run in threads with direct in-memory parameter exchange (eliminating serialization and IPC overhead), while each client uses a dedicated, framework-managed RNG stream. This ensures bitwise-identical results across repeated high-concurrency runs—both in thread-based and process-based modes—under fixed software/hardware stacks. On CIFAR-10 classification, BlazeFL achieves up to **3.1× speedup** over a widely used open-source baseline for communication-dominated workloads, while maintaining a minimal dependency footprint. The open-source implementation is available at https://github.com/kitsuyaazuma/blazefl.

item.title: Your Agent is More Brittle Than You Think: Uncovering Indirect Injection Vulnerabilities in Agentic LLMs
item.description: This paper uncovers critical fragility in modern agentic LLMs under Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) attacks—where malicious instructions are stealthily embedded in third-party content (e.g., API responses, web scrapes) and trigger unauthorized actions during normal tool-calling workflows. Moving beyond static, single-turn benchmarks, we conduct the first systematic evaluation of six defense strategies against four sophisticated IPI vectors across nine open-weight LLM backbones—*entirely within dynamic, multi-step tool-execution environments*. Our multidimensional analysis reveals that advanced IPIs bypass nearly all baseline defenses, and some mitigations even degrade reliability. Crucially, while agents execute malicious instructions rapidly, their internal representations at tool-input positions exhibit abnormally high decision entropy—indicating latent hesitation. Leveraging this signal, we propose a Representation Engineering (RepE)-based circuit breaker that extracts hidden states *just before tool invocation* to detect and intercept unauthorized actions in real time. It achieves **94.2% average detection accuracy** across models with zero fine-tuning or external supervision—establishing a practical, deployment-ready paradigm for resilient multi-agent systems.

item.title: Reformulating the SNOVA Signature Scheme
item.description: This note reformulates the SNOVA signature scheme by unifying its three existing representations—ring-equation, whipping, and tensor forms—into a coherent, modular framework. Our reformulation explicitly decouples noise sampling, structural masking, and linearization, enabling fine-grained, independent control over ring dimension, tensor order, noise distribution, and field characteristic. Security is preserved under standard assumptions (Ring-LWE and low-rank tensor indistinguishability), with enhanced provability due to structural transparency. Crucially, parameter sets over odd-characteristic fields $\mathbb{F}_q$ yield **smaller keys (18–25% reduction)** and **improved resistance against algebraic and lattice-based attacks**, making them practically compelling. We propose **five concrete parameter sets** with full signature certificates (signature + public key) **below 1 KB**, the smallest being 923 bytes—demonstrating strong suitability for resource-constrained post-quantum deployment.

item.title: Delegate: Coalition Proof Incentivized Outsourced Computation with Smart Contracts
item.description: We propose **Delegate**, the first smart contract-based multi-contractor incentivized outsourced computation protocol with *provable coalition-proof security*. Unlike prior works—limited to two contractors or lacking formal security against fully colluding coalitions—Delegate ensures that honest computation remains the unique strong Nash equilibrium *even when any subset of contractors arbitrarily coordinates*, under standard cryptographic assumptions. To eliminate the copy attack—a critical threat where malicious contractors plagiarize others’ responses—we design a *universally composable (UC) response submission protocol* based on time-locked commitments and NIZK proofs, rendering copying *information-theoretically impossible* before revelation, not merely disincentivized. Delegate achieves efficient on-chain execution via off-chain aggregation and minimal on-chain verification (e.g., hash checks), supporting arbitrary $n \geq 2$ contractors on EVM-compatible blockchains. Our formal analysis confirms UC security in the F_{commit} hybrid model, establishing a new foundation for trustless, scalable outsourced computation.

item.title: Game Theory Does Not Always Help: The Case of Statistical Multi-Party Coin Tossing
item.description: This paper resolves a fundamental open question: whether game-theoretic fairness—via equilibrium concepts like Nash or subgame-perfect equilibrium—can overcome the statistical impossibility of multi-party coin tossing when no honest majority exists. We prove *negative* results in two key settings: (1) With broadcast, no statistically secure, game-theoretically fair coin-tossing protocol exists for $n$ parties tolerating $t \geq n/2$ corruptions and bounded rounds—except the isolated case $n=4$, where three parties share the same preference. This complements known feasibility for $t < n/2$, completing the statistical feasibility landscape. (2) Without broadcast (point-to-point only), no computationally secure game-theoretic protocol exists for $t \geq n/3$ and polynomial round complexity—except $n=6$ with five identical preferences. Our proofs rely on a refined framework for modeling game-theoretic fairness that explicitly captures communication primitives (broadcast vs. point-to-point), enabling tight lower bounds. Crucially, our results show that game theory provides *no advantage* over classical cryptography in the statistical setting once an honest majority is lost—settling the limits of equilibrium-based fairness in coin tossing.

item.title: Improved Codes and Decoders for HQC
item.description: The Hamming Quasi-Cyclic (HQC) cryptosystem—recently selected by NIST for standardization—relies on a concatenated Reed-Muller (RM) and Reed-Solomon (RS) code for reliable decryption. This work reduces ciphertext and public-key sizes while rigorously maintaining the target decryption failure rate (DFR < 2⁻¹²⁸). First, we design a two-level generalized concatenated code (GCC) leveraging a repetition subcode inherent in the inner RM code; this enables a higher-rate outer RS code without compromising DFR. Second, we introduce a reliability-based outer decoding framework: the inner decoder outputs both hard decisions and analytically bounded reliability metrics, whose joint distribution with decoding outcomes yields conservative DFR guarantees for both threshold- and partition-based erasure assignment. Combined, these improvements shrink public-key and ciphertext sizes by up to **4.34%**, verified across NIST Round 4 parameter sets, with no change to security assumptions or implementation complexity.

item.title: Fast and Efficient Perfectly Secure Network-Agnostic Secure Computation
item.description: We present the first *network-agnostic* perfectly secure MPC protocol achieving *expected* round complexity $O(D)$ and expected communication complexity $O((Cn^2 + Dn^2 + n^4)\log n)$ for circuits of size $C$ and depth $D$ with $n$ parties, tolerating $t < n/3$ malicious corruptions. Unlike prior art—including recent Eurocrypt’23/’24 protocols that require precise network-type knowledge—our construction guarantees correctness and perfect security *simultaneously* in both synchronous and asynchronous networks, without assumptions or switching logic. Technically, we depart from dual-mode designs and introduce novel primitives: an adaptive gate-evaluation framework, a lightweight asynchronous agreement primitive resilient to timing uncertainty, and a layered error-correcting structure that enables efficient information dispersal under worst-case scheduling. Our protocol improves over the state of the art by a factor of $\Theta(n^3)$ in communication for small circuits and $\Theta(n^2)$ for large ones, plus an additive $O(n)$ reduction in round complexity. This resolves a central open problem in foundational MPC.

item.title: Learning the Signature of Memorization in Autoregressive Language Models
item.description: We introduce **Learned Transfer MIA (LT-MIA)**, the first transferable, end-to-end learned membership inference attack for fine-tuned language models—eliminating reliance on hand-crafted heuristics and shadow models. Leveraging the fact that fine-tuning inherently provides unlimited labeled data (membership is known by construction), LT-MIA learns a universal signature of memorization directly from transformer-based models and transfers *zero-shot* to architecturally distinct families: Mamba (state-space), RWKV-4 (linear attention), and RecurrentGemma (gated recurrence), achieving AUCs of **0.972**, **0.963**, and **0.936**, respectively—all surpassing held-out transformer performance (0.908 AUC). Remarkably, this signature persists across domains: LT-MIA generalizes to code (0.865 AUC) despite training only on natural language. On transformers, it attains **2.8× higher TPR at 0.1% FPR** versus the strongest baseline. The success of even simple likelihood baselines further confirms the existence of a fundamental, architecture-agnostic memorization signal rooted solely in gradient descent on cross-entropy loss. Code and models are publicly available.

item.title: A Systematic Security Evaluation of OpenClaw and Its Variants
item.description: This paper presents the first systematic security evaluation of six OpenClaw-series agent frameworks—OpenClaw, AutoClaw, QClaw, KimiClaw, MaxClaw, and ArkClaw—across multiple backbone LLMs (e.g., Llama-3, Qwen2, DeepSeek-V2). We introduce a benchmark of 205 realistic test cases covering reconnaissance, credential leakage, lateral movement, privilege escalation, and resource development across the full agent execution lifecycle. Results show that *all* evaluated agents exhibit severe, framework-specific vulnerabilities—reconnaissance behaviors are the most prevalent weakness (41%), and agentized systems pose significantly higher risks than their underlying LLMs used in isolation. Critically, early-stage flaws (e.g., lax tool parameter validation) amplify through multi-step planning and persistent context into concrete system-level failures. Our findings demonstrate that agent security is governed not just by model safety, but by the tight coupling of model capability, tool interface design, planning logic, and runtime orchestration—urging a shift from prompt-level safeguards to lifecycle-wide security governance.
